what_it_does: Declares a single RELATION_TYPE dict for a 'sourced_from' board edge: a directed edge from a board item to the source-type row it originated from (e.g. claude_code, a future github), described as the seam the GitHub fold-in joins on via author/path/time.
observations: Module-level constant RELATION_TYPE only; no functions or classes.
Fields: id, directed (True), label ('sourced-from'), desc; no inverse field.
desc gives the target as a source-type row and names claude_code as a current source and github as a future one.
desc names the join keys for a future GitHub fold-in: author, path, time.
standouts: Names a future GitHub integration ('the GitHub fold-in') and the author/path/time join seam it would use.
conventions: directed=True without an inverse, like same_law in this batch.
notes: A provenance edge tying a board item to its origin source-type row; doubles as the planned join seam for folding in GitHub data.
questions: What is a 'source-type row' (which table/registry) that this points into?
Is the GitHub fold-in implemented anywhere, or purely planned per this desc?
purpose_vs_actual: Match: the file declares exactly the sourced-from provenance edge its purpose states.
intention_signals: Aims to record where each board item came from as a typed edge, and to pre-shape the seam (author/path/time) on which future external sources like GitHub can be joined in.
novelty: Encodes both current provenance and a planned external-ingestion join key inside one relation-type declaration.
contribution: Contributes the sourced-from provenance edge definition linking board items to their origin source-type and naming the author/path/time join for external sources.
summary_for_embedding: A board_edges relation-type declaration defining the directed sourced_from edge that links a board item to its origin source-type row (claude_code, future github), the join seam for the GitHub fold-in.
intention_for_embedding: To track board-item provenance as a typed edge and pre-shape the author/path/time seam for folding in external sources like GitHub.
